question: At what height is fall protection required for construction workers?
expected_answer: Fall protection is required when workers are exposed to a fall of 6 feet or more to a lower level.

question: How often must fire extinguishers be inspected and serviced?
expected_answer: Fire extinguishers must be kept fully charged, inspected monthly, and serviced annually.

question: How much water must employers provide to employees for heat illness prevention?
expected_answer: Employers must provide at least one quart of fresh, pure, suitably cool potable water per employee per hour for the entire shift.

question: How often must supervisors conduct tailgate or toolbox safety meetings?
expected_answer: Supervisors must conduct tailgate or toolbox safety meetings at least every 10 working days, though weekly meetings are recommended.

question: What are the four soil classification categories used for excavation protective systems?
expected_answer: The four categories are Stable Rock, Type A soil, Type B soil, and Type C soil.

question: At what temperature must hot pipes and surfaces be covered or guarded?
expected_answer: Hot pipes and surfaces must be covered with thermal insulating material or guarded when they are at 140°F (60°C) or higher and located within 7 feet vertically from the floor or working level, or 15 inches horizontally from stairways, ramps, or fixed ladders.

question: What is the minimum height at which guardrails must be installed on open work surfaces?
expected_answer: Guardrails must be installed at the open sides of all work surfaces that are 7.5 feet or higher above the ground, floor, or level underneath, or workers must be protected by other fall protection means.

question: When must an elevator be provided at a construction site?
expected_answer: An elevator is required for structures or buildings 60 feet or more above ground level or 48 feet below ground level when completed. It must be installed when the structure reaches 36 feet in height.

question: What must a written heat illness prevention plan include at a minimum?
expected_answer: The plan must include procedures for providing sufficient water, procedures for providing access to shade, high-heat procedures, emergency response procedures, and acclimatization methods and procedures.

question: What are the three types of protective systems permitted for excavations to protect workers from cave-ins?
expected_answer: The three types are sloping/benching, shoring (standard shoring per 1540 and 1541.1), and trench shields. Protective system design must be based on soil classification.

question: What are the minimum strength requirements for a guardrail top rail?
expected_answer: All railings and components must be capable of withstanding a force of at least 200 lbs. applied to the top rail within 2 inches of the top edge, in any outward or downward direction, at any point along the top edge.

question: What are the key elements required in a written Injury and Illness Prevention Program (IIPP)?
expected_answer: The IIPP must include: assignment of responsibilities, a system for ensuring employee compliance with safe work practices, a system for two-way communication about safety, scheduled inspections and hazard evaluation, accident investigation process, procedures for correcting unsafe conditions, safety and health training, employee access to the IIPP, and recordkeeping.

question: What are the requirements for a Personal Fall Arrest (PFA) system?
expected_answer: A PFA system must limit the maximum arresting force on an employee to 1,800 lbs., be rigged so the employee cannot free-fall more than 6 feet or contact any lower level, and have anchorage points able to support 5,000 lbs. per employee attached. The use of a body belt as part of a PFA system is prohibited.

question: What high-heat procedures must employers implement when temperature reaches 95°F?
expected_answer: Employers must implement high-heat procedures including: training all employees to recognize heat illness signs and symptoms, ensuring employees stay in contact and observe each other, monitoring employees using buddy systems or regular communication, reminding employees throughout the shift to drink plenty of water, providing close supervision to new employees, and conducting pre-shift meetings to review procedures.

question: What permit requirements apply to excavations in California?
expected_answer: A DOSH permit is required before workers enter excavations that are 5 feet deep or deeper. Also, for excavations deeper than 20 feet, protective systems must be designed by a registered engineer.

question: What are the lock-out/block-out requirements before cleaning, repairing, or adjusting machinery?
expected_answer: Machinery capable of movement must be stopped and the power source de-energized or disengaged. Moveable parts must be mechanically blocked or locked out. Accident prevention signs or tags must be placed on the controls. A written hazardous energy control procedure must be developed and used. For equipment with lockable controls, it must be locked out or sealed in the off position.

question: A worker is preparing to enter a permit-required confined space. What must the employer do before allowing entry?
expected_answer: The employer must have a competent person identify all confined spaces, post danger signs informing employees of permit spaces, comply with all applicable Cal/OSHA regulations, implement a written permit space program that includes: procedures to identify and evaluate permit spaces, measures to prevent unauthorized entry, a system for issuing and canceling entry permits, procedures to test and monitor atmospheric and physical hazards, an attendant posted outside at all times, appropriate equipment (monitoring, ventilation, PPE, lighting, rescue), employee and supervisor training, and effective on-site rescue procedures.

question: What respiratory protection is required for different lead trigger task levels in construction?
expected_answer: Level 1 tasks (spray painting, manual demolition, manual scraping/sanding, heat gun, power tool cleaning with dust collection) require a half-mask respirator with N100, R100, or P100 filters. Level 2 tasks (lead mortar, burning lead, rivet busting, cleaning power tools without dust collection, dry abrasive cleanup, removing abrasive blasting enclosure) require a full-face mask with N100/R100/P100, or supplied-air hood/helmet, or loose-fitting hood with PAPR. Level 3 tasks (abrasive blasting, welding, cutting, or torch burning on structures) require a half-mask supplied-air respirator in positive pressure mode.

question: What fall protection requirements apply to workers on roofs with slopes exceeding 4:12 and more than 20 feet in height?
expected_answer: For single-unit roofs with slopes exceeding 4:12 and more than 20 feet in height, employees must be protected from falls by one or more of the following: parapets at least 24 inches high, personal fall protection systems, catch platforms, scaffold platforms, eave barriers, or standard railings and toeboards. For multi-unit roofs with slopes exceeding 5:12 and more than 20 feet, similar options apply with the addition of roof jack systems (safety lines required when slope exceeds 7:12).

question: What are the required steps an employer must take before opening an excavation at a work site?
expected_answer: Before opening an excavation, the employer must: identify subsurface installations and ensure they are marked, notify all regional notification centers and subsurface installation owners at least two working days before starting work, receive a positive response from all known owners/operators, meet with owners/operators of high-priority installations within 10 feet of the proposed excavation, ensure only qualified persons perform subsurface locating activities, train all exposed employees in excavator notification and excavation activities, and obtain a DOSH permit if workers will enter an excavation 5 feet deep or deeper.
